title: The Late Preterm Infant
authors: Arnold HE, Parsons KV, Jain L / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Late Preterm Infant?
ทารก late preterm (34 0/7 – 36 6/7 wk) คิดเป็น 73% of all preterm births (6.3% of all US live births) — มากที่สุดในกลุ่ม preterm ทั้งหมด. ปัญหาหลักคือ: (1) 'ดูใหญ่เหมือน term' แต่สรีรวิทยายังไม่สมบูรณ์ → ทีมมักประเมิน risk ต่ำไป → พลาด hypoglycemia, jaundice, feeding failure, sepsis. (2) Infant mortality 5x สูงกว่า 39–40 wk (8.2 vs 1.5 per 1000, US 2019). (3) Rehospitalization 4.4% vs 2% term — ส่วนใหญ่จาก jaundice (63–71%) + feeding difficulties. (4) Brain weight ที่ 34 wk = 65% ของ term; cerebral volume = 53% → neurodevelopmental delay ระยะยาว: ↓ IQ, ↑ behavioral problems, ↓ academic performance. (5) ALPS 2016: betamethasone ลด respiratory complications ได้แม้ที่ 34–36 6/7 wk → เปลี่ยน obstetric practice ไปแล้ว. รู้จัก late preterm = รู้จักผู้ป่วยที่ดูเหมือน 'โอเค' แต่เสี่ยงจริง.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
นิยาม late preterm ตาม NICHD 2005: 34 0/7 – 36 6/7 wk; แยก early term, full term, late term, post-term ตาม WHO/AAP/ACOG definition
วิเคราะห์ epidemiology: อัตราในสหรัฐฯ (6.3% ของ live births), racial disparity, trend ตั้งแต่ 2007–2019
อธิบาย etiologies ของ late preterm birth: medical indications vs iatrogenic causes (C/S, induction), ART, advanced maternal age

อธิบาย pathophysiology ทุก organ system: thermoregulation (brown fat ↓), feeding (oromotor immaturity), glucose homeostasis (ENaC + glycogen), neurologic immaturity (brain 65%), respiratory (ENaC ↓, ↑ TTN/RDS/PPHN)
ระบุ clinical outcomes ตาม GA: RDS/TTN/PPHN rates by week; mortality rates (Young 2007); rehospitalization pattern
อธิบาย hyperbilirubinemia ใน late preterm: mechanism, risk (8x สูงกว่า term ที่ 36 wk), kernicterus risk
เข้าใจ long-term neurodevelopmental outcomes: IQ, behavioral problems, academic failure, disability data
ระบุ management: admission criteria, monitoring, feeding plan (ESPGHAN 2019), discharge criteria (12 criteria + 48-hour minimum)
ประยุกต์ใช้ AAP hypoglycemia algorithm ใน late preterm: glucose thresholds ตาม age, dextrose gel evidence (Sugar Babies Study 2013)
เชื่อม late preterm birth กับ NICU workload: ป้องกัน kernicterus, reduce rehospitalization, ทำ proper discharge planning
1. Definition and Classification
1.1 WHO / AAP / ACOG Definitions
WHO 1950: preterm = <37 completed weeks (counting from first day of LMP = day 1, delivery before day 259)
NICHD Expert Panel July 2005: coined term 'late preterm' for 34 0/7 – 36 6/7 weeks (days 239–259); previously called 'near-term' — changed to avoid false sense of maturity

rows: Focus of this chapter — 73% of all preterm births
Also at ↑ risk vs 39–40 wk; OR adverse outcome 1.5 (37 wk), 1.5 (38 wk) vs 39 wk

blocks.text: 1.2 Why 34 Weeks as the Lower Cutoff?
34 wk ถือเป็น maturational milestone ใน obstetric practice: surfactant มักเพียงพอในปอด → historically ถูกมองว่าเป็น 'safe' gestational age
Historical practice: antenatal steroids + tocolysis ให้เฉพาะก่อน 34 wk (เปลี่ยนแปลงหลัง ALPS trial 2016)
Misconception ที่ต้องแก้: 34 wk ≠ mature; มีความเสี่ยงสูงกว่า term อย่างมีนัยสำคัญ
2. Epidemiology
2.1 United States Data
2020 US: 3,613,647 live births; preterm 10.09% (≈365,000); late preterm 6.3% = 73% of all preterm births (Osterman 2022)
Trend: preterm birth rate ↓ 2007→2014 (nadir 9.57%) → ↑ again 2014→2019 (10.23%) — recent increase of concern
Decrease 2007–2014: attributed to national quality improvement programs to ↓ elective deliveries before 39 wk
2.2 Racial Disparities
Non-Hispanic Black: 9.54% late preterm rate vs 7.32% Hispanic vs 6.9% non-Hispanic White
Non-Hispanic Black infants: nearly 1.4x the late preterm birth rate of non-Hispanic White infants — persistent disparity
2.3 Mortality
Mortality rate at 34 wk: ~12 per 1000 live births vs ~2 per 1000 at 39 wk (Reddy 2009)
Infant mortality 5x higher for late preterm vs 39–40 wk: 8.2 vs 1.5/1000 (US 2019, Ely 2021)
Swedish cohort (675,000 singleton births 1973–1979): late preterm → ↑ mortality at 18–36 years of age (AHR 1.53 early childhood; 1.31 young adulthood)
3. Etiology of Late Preterm Birth

Late Preterm Birth — Etiologies Overview (Box 41.1)
Medically indicated: preterm labor, PROM, preeclampsia. Iatrogenic (medical interventions): C/S rate ↑ from 5% (1970) → 32.9% (2009) → 31.8% (2020); elective C/S + induction on maternal request; 2.5% of C/S = maternal request (ACOG CO 559, 2013). 36% of elective repeat C/S performed before 39 wk (Tita NEJM 2009). Induction rates ↑ 130% since 1990 for late preterm births. Advanced maternal age + ART: ART 2014: 57,323 deliveries; 32% multiples; 57% twins born preterm; 98.7% triplets+ born preterm. Gestational age assessment errors: Naegele rule ± 1–2 wk; US overestimation in macrosomia (obesity, GDM).
3.1 Tita Table — Adverse Outcomes by GA at Elective C/S
Tita ATN et al NEJM 2009;360:111: outcomes in 24,077 elective repeat C/S; reference group = 39 wk
4. Pathophysiology — Organ System Immaturity
4.1 Thermoregulation
Nonshivering thermogenesis: hypothalamic ventral medial nucleus → sympathetic → norepinephrine → brown adipose tissue (BAT) → free fatty acids → oxidation → heat
Late preterm: ↓ BAT stores; ↓ hormones for brown fat metabolism (prolactin, norepinephrine, T3, cortisol — all peak at term gestation)
↓ white adipose tissue → ↓ insulation; ↑ surface area : body weight ratio → rapid heat loss
Cold stress → tachypnea, poor color (peripheral vasoconstriction), altered pulmonary vascular tone, metabolic acidosis → can mimic sepsis → unnecessary workups
Temperature instability: 10% vs 0% (P<.0012) in late preterm vs term (Wang 2004); 5.2% of late preterm NICU admissions had hypothermia as primary reason
4.2 Feeding
Neuronal immaturity → poor sucking-swallowing coordination; ↓ oromotor tone → lower intraoral pressures; immature deglutition, sphincter control, peristalsis
Poor caloric intake → dehydration → exacerbates physiologic jaundice → rehospitalization
↓ hepatic UDP-glucuronyl transferase activity + ↑ enterohepatic circulation → ↓ bilirubin conjugation → prolonged, prevalent, severe jaundice
Jaundice at 36 wk: 8x ↑ risk TSB >20 mg/dL vs ≥41 wk infants (Newman 1999)
Feeding failure = primary reason for prolonged hospital stay: 76% of prolonged stay; IV fluids needed 27% vs 5% term (OR 6.48; Wang 2004)
4.3 Glucose Homeostasis

Late Preterm Hypoglycemia — Mechanisms
Multiple mechanisms act in concert: (1) Immature hepatic enzymes for glycogenolysis + gluconeogenesis; (2) Hormonal dysregulation: ↓ catecholamine/glucagon surge; immature pancreatic β-cell → unregulated insulin secretion during hypoglycemia; (3) ↓ hepatic glycogen stores (depleted rapidly after birth); (4) ↓ adipose tissue lipolysis; (5) Delayed feeds or low volume intake. Incidence: 3–4x higher than term, especially first 12 hours (Hosagasi 2017). Severe hypoglycemia → neuronal cell death → ↑ risk poor executive function + visual motor function at 4.5 years (McKinlay JAMA Pediatr 2017).
4.4 Neurologic Immaturity
Brain weight at 34 wk = 65% of term; cerebral volume = 53% of term (Billiards 2006; Khashu 2009)
Cerebral cortex still smooth at late preterm — gyri + sulci not fully formed; myelination + interneuronal connectivity incomplete
Brain continues to grow until 2 years of age → 80% of adult brain volume
Multiple insults during neuronal/glial maturation → white + gray matter injuries, particularly thalamic region + periventricular white matter
IVH: RR 4.9 vs term; cerebral palsy: RR 3.1 vs term (Teune 2011, meta-analysis)
4.5 Respiratory — ENaC and Lung Fluid Clearance

Fetal Lung Fluid Clearance — ENaC Pathway (Jain L, Am J Perinatol 2008)
3-stage developmental model: (1) Fetal stage: lung epithelium in secretory mode — active Cl⁻ secretion via Cl⁻ channels, low Na⁺ reabsorption. (2) Transitional stage at birth: reversal of ion/water movement — ↑ Na⁺ channel expression (nonselective → highly selective ENaC); ↓ Cl⁻ secretion. Triggered by: air-interface exposure, ↑ steroids, ↑ cyclic nucleotides. (3) Adult stage: predominantly Na⁺ reabsorption via ENaC + Na,K-ATPase. Post-birth clearance: 2-step: Na⁺ passive entry (apical ENaC) → active extrusion (basolateral Na,K-ATPase) → osmotic gradient → water follows. ENaC expression peaks at term — late preterm ↓ ENaC → ↓ lung fluid clearance → TTN/RDS. Glucocorticoids: ↑ ENaC transcription + ↓ channel degradation + ↑ channel activity → mechanism of antenatal steroids.
RDS incidence by GA: 10.5% at 34 wk → 0.3% at 38 wk (Consortium on Safe Labor, JAMA 2010)
Respiratory support needed: 30%/33%/23% at 34/35/36 wk (Clark 2005)
Mechanical ventilation: 3.3–6.3% at 34 wk; 1.7–3.6% at 35 wk; 0.8–2.3% at 36 wk
Late preterm in ECMO registry (ELSO, 14,500 infants 1986–2006): 14.7% were late preterm; ↑ PPHN + RDS as primary causes; ↑ mortality OR 2.73 vs term
5. Clinical Outcomes by System
5.1 Mortality Data
Teune 2011 (systematic review, 2000–2010): late preterm 4x more likely to die in first year than term
Severe morbidity meta-analysis (Teune 2011): RDS RR 17.3; IVH RR 4.9; death <28d RR 5.9; cerebral palsy RR 3.1
5.2 Respiratory Morbidity
British Columbia Registry: late preterm had 4.4x respiratory morbidity and 5.2x neonatal infection rate vs term; LOS 142 vs 57 h (Khashu 2009)
Wang 2004: RDS 28.9% vs 4.2% (OR 9.14); apnea 4.4% vs 0% in late preterm vs term (despite similar size + Apgar)
PPHN: higher incidence than term; more severe: ↑ ECMO need; ↑ complications on ECMO (OR 2.73 mortality)
Long-term: ↑ asthma, ↓ lung function later in life (Clark 2005; Goyal 2011; Kotecha 2012)
5.3 Hospitalization and Rehospitalization
NICU admission rates: 97% (34 wk), 53% (35 wk), 32% (36 wk) from large population-based study
Hospital stay: 6–11 days (34 wk); 4–6 days (35 wk); 3–4 days (36 wk)
Most common NICU admission reasons: jaundice, RDS, dehydration/poor feeding, hypoglycemia, temperature instability
Rehospitalization rate: 4.4% late preterm vs 2% term (Escobar 2005); even higher risk in never-NICU group (3x risk of readmission) → clinical lapse in risk recognition
Reasons for readmission: hyperbilirubinemia 63–71%, feeding difficulties 16%, suspected sepsis 6–20% (Shapiro-Mendoza 2006)
Economic burden: relative direct cost 2.9x vs term infant; total comparable to extremely preterm cohort due to large volume (IOM 2007: $51,600/preterm birth; $26B total US 2005)
6. Hyperbilirubinemia
6.1 Mechanism and Risk
2.4x more likely to develop significant hyperbilirubinemia vs term (Sarici 2004)
Dual mechanisms: (1) ↓ hepatic UDP-glucuronyl transferase → ↓ conjugation; (2) ↑ enterohepatic circulation (immature GI motility)
36 wk infants: 8x ↑ risk TSB >20 mg/dL vs ≥41 wk infants (Newman 1999)
Bhutani & Johnson (Kernicterus Registry): late preterm at ↑ risk of kernicterus at bilirubin EQUAL TO OR LESS THAN levels causing kernicterus in term infants
Pattern: more prolonged, more pronounced, more protracted than term — often breastfeeding without adequate lactation support
Rehospitalization for jaundice: 4.5% vs 1.2% in term (Escobar 2005)
6.2 Clinical Warning

Kernicterus Risk in Late Preterm — 'Deceptively Large' Trap
Danger: larger size of late preterm infants may fool practitioners into treating them as term → complacency around jaundice thresholds. Bhutani + Johnson: nearly ALL late preterm infants in Kernicterus Registry were breastfeeding without adequate lactation support + scheduled for 2-wk follow-up. Rule: (1) Pre-discharge bilirubin (serum or TcB) in ALL late preterm before discharge; (2) Use bilirubin nomograms with late preterm thresholds (NOT term thresholds); (3) Follow-up within 24–48 hours (not 2 weeks) after discharge; (4) Lactation support mandatory.
7. Immunity and Infection
7.1 Sepsis Workup Pattern
Sepsis workup 36.7% vs 12.6% term (OR 3.97, P<.00015); antibiotics more often + longer duration (30% 7-day course vs 17% term) — (Vachharajani 2008)
Workup rate by GA: 33% at 34 wk vs 12% at 39 wk (P<.01) — but culture-proven sepsis only 0.4%
Root cause: late preterm clinical signs (RDS, cold stress, hypoglycemia) mimic sepsis → 'defensive workup' → unnecessary antibiotics
British Columbia: 5.2x neonatal infection rate vs term — some from true infection; most from workup culture results
8. Long-term Neurodevelopmental Outcomes

Neurodevelopmental Consequences of Late Preterm Birth
Woythaler 2011 (ECLS-B cohort): late preterm ↑ OR for MDI <70 (OR 1.51; 95% CI 1.26–1.82) + PDI <70 (OR 1.56; 95% CI 1.29–1.88) at 24 months. Woythaler 2015: still delayed at kindergarten: worse Total School Readiness Score. Talge 2010: 336 matched pairs; late preterm at 6 years: ↓ IQ + ↑ teacher-reported behavioral problems. Millennium Cohort Study (18,800 infants, UK): late preterm → ↓ personal/social/emotional, communication/language, literacy, math development at 5 years. Townley Flores 2021 (73,000 children California): moderate + late preterm (32–36 6/7 wk) → ↑ low performance math/English, ↑ chronic absenteeism, ↑ suspension — grades K–5, suspension risk persists to grade 12. Lindstrom 2007 (500,000 Swedish adults): late preterm → ↑ disability assistance; ↓ likelihood of university/postsecondary education.
Chyi 2008 (ECLS-K): ↓ direct child assessment scores + teacher academic rating scales; ↑ IEP + special education enrollment
Swedish cohort: ↑ mortality at 18–36 years — effects not limited to neonatal period
Caution: retrospective studies — causality uncertain; neurologic injury may predate or result from the event causing late preterm birth
9. Management — Admission, Monitoring, Feeding
9.1 Admission Criteria

AAP Admission + Hospital Management Guidelines (Engle 2007, Box 41.2)
Admit: <35 wk gestation OR <2300 g birth weight to closely monitored area. 'Well' late preterm in mother's room (first 24h): only if close monitoring available. Transfer to mother's room: only when stability confirmed: temperature, vital signs, blood glucose, oral feeding. Gestational age assessment: use obstetric estimate if based on 1st trimester US; if discrepancy >2 wk between OB estimate + newborn exam → use newborn exam GA. Transfer to NICU: if FiO₂ >0.4 needed.
9.2 Monitoring Protocol
Physical exam on admission AND at discharge; vital signs + SpO₂ on admission
Vital signs every 3–4 h first 24h → every shift thereafter
Glucose screening per AAP protocol (high-risk group): initial feed within 1h; screen 30 min after 1st feed
Breastfeeding assessment formally documented by trained caregiver at least 2x daily
9.3 Hypoglycemia Management — AAP Algorithm (Adamkin 2013)

Dextrose gel (40% dextrose, buccal): Sugar Babies Study (Harris DL Lancet 2013; RCT): inexpensive, easy; ↓ IV fluids + ↓ NICU admissions; recommended first-line for late preterm + term hypoglycemic infants <24h
Subsequent studies (Gregory 2020): dextrose gel + feeding → ↓ IV infusion need. Not yet in current AAP guideline but many institutions now include in local algorithms
9.4 Feeding Recommendations (ESPGHAN 2019)
ESPGHAN Committee on Nutrition (Lapillone 2019): breastfeeding strongly encouraged; individualized feeding plan for oromotor immaturity
Caloric supplementation: consider depending on GA, birth weight, comorbidities
Iron supplementation: 1–2 mg/kg/day for <2500 g for at least 6 months
Vitamin D: 400 IU/day throughout early childhood
3/4 of late preterm require feeding support; 27% need IV fluids vs 5% term (Wang 2004)
9.5 Discharge Criteria

12-Point Discharge Checklist for Late Preterm Infants (Engle 2007 / Box 41.2)
Minimum stay: ≥48 hours after birth. Vital signs stable 12h: RR <60/min, HR 100–160/min, axillary T 36.5–37.4°C in open crib. Feeding: ≥24h successful oral feeding with coordinated suck-swallow-breathe. Weight loss >7% in 48h → delay discharge for further assessment. Passage of ≥1 stool spontaneously; adequate urine output. Jaundice: pre-discharge bilirubin (serum/TcB); TcB >12 mg → confirm with serum; bilirubin nomogram for risk + follow-up need. No active circumcision bleeding ≥2h. Hep B #1 given or appointment scheduled. Metabolic/genetic screening done. CCHD screen passed; car-seat safety test; hearing assessment documented. Social risk assessment; if risk factors → delay until care plan generated. Follow-up appointment with physician 24–48h after discharge. Caregiver competency demonstrated: urine/stool frequency, cord care, signs of illness, jaundice instructions, sleep position, thermometer use, emergency response.
10. Algorithm — Late Preterm Management Flow

rows: Confirm GA (obstetric estimate vs newborn exam); if >2 wk discrepancy → use newborn exam GA
<35 wk or <2300 g → monitored area; 35–36 6/7 wk + well → mother's room only if close monitoring available
Vital signs + SpO₂; feed within 1h; glucose screen 30 min after first feed; temperature management (warm environment, skin-to-skin if possible)
If FiO₂ >0.4 → transfer NICU; watch for subtle TTN, apnea, PPHN signs; SpO₂ monitoring
AAP algorithm: <25 mg/dL (0–4h) or <35 mg/dL (4–24h) → refeed ± dextrose gel ± IV; target ≥45 mg/dL before routine feeds
Individualized; breastfeeding assessment 2x/day by trained staff; supplement if weight loss >7% or inadequate intake; monitor suck-swallow coordination
TcB or serum bilirubin before discharge; nomogram-based risk assessment; follow-up 24–48h post-discharge
Workup only if clinical signs beyond expected physiology; avoid reflexive workup for normal transition signs
All 12 criteria met; minimum 48h; caregiver education complete; follow-up scheduled
24–48h follow-up mandatory; reassess feeding, weight, jaundice; reinforce lactation support

blocks.text: 11. NICU Practical — Bedside Application
11.1 Respiratory Support
TTN: CPAP 5–6 cmH₂O; FiO₂ titrate; ส่วนใหญ่ resolve ใน 24–72h; ถ้า FiO₂ >0.4 → NICU admission; ถ้า clinical PPHN → iNO
RDS: surfactant ถ้า FiO₂ >0.3–0.4 บน CPAP; INSURE (INtubate-SURfactant-Extubate) or LISA (Less Invasive Surfactant Administration) ตาม unit protocol
PPHN in late preterm: ↑ risk เทียบ term; iNO ≥20 ppm หาก oxygenation index >20–25; high-frequency oscillation อาจจำเป็น
11.2 Temperature
เป้าหมาย axillary 36.5–37.4°C; ใช้ radiant warmer ± incubator จนกว่า infant จะ thermoregulate ในเสื้อผ้า/ผ้าห่มได้
Skin-to-skin: ช่วย thermal regulation + breastfeeding initiation; แต่ต้อง monitor breathing อย่างใกล้ชิด — late preterm เสี่ยง apnea during skin-to-skin
11.3 Hypoglycemia Protocol
Feed ก่อนเสมอ (breast or formula) ก่อน IV; dextrose gel 0.5 mL/kg 40% dextrose บริเวณ buccal mucosa + feed ตามทันที
IV dextrose rescue: 200 mg/kg = D10W 2 mL/kg bolus IV; GIR เริ่ม 4–6 mg/kg/min; titrate
หยุด glucose screen เมื่อ glucose ≥45 mg/dL ใน 2 consecutive feeds หรือตาม unit protocol
11.4 Jaundice
TcB ทุก late preterm ก่อน discharge; ถ้า TcB >12 mg/dL → serum bilirubin
ใช้ Bhutani nomogram (high risk zone สำหรับ late preterm) — threshold phototherapy ต่ำกว่า term infant
Follow-up 24–48h หลัง discharge; ถ้า discharge <72h → bilirubin check ที่ clinic ใน 24h
Breastfeeding support ต่อเนื่อง: poor intake → jaundice + dehydration cycle → kernicterus
11.5 Infection
Clinical signs ที่ควร distinguish จาก normal physiology: persistent hypothermia (T <36.5 after warming), persistent hypoglycemia, respiratory distress beyond 4h, poor perfusion
ถ้า workup ทำ: CBC + blood culture + CRP; LP ถ้า clinical sepsis; antibiotics ตาม unit protocol
ระวัง over-diagnosis: TTN + cold stress + hypoglycemia อาจ mimic sepsis โดยสิ้นเชิง
12. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — 'ดูใหญ่' ≠ 'สมบูรณ์': Brain ยังขาดอีก 35–47%
ที่ 34 wk: brain weight = 65%, cerebral volume = 53% ของ term. Gyri/sulci ยังไม่สมบูรณ์; myelination incomplete; periventricular white matter เสี่ยง injury. Clinical implication: late preterm ที่ดูเหมือน 'โอเค' ทางกาย — แต่สมองยังอ่อนแอมาก. อย่าใช้ขนาดตัวเป็น surrogate ของ maturity. IVH RR 4.9; cerebral palsy RR 3.1 vs term (Teune 2011).

PEARL 2 — Jaundice: 8x Risk at 36 Weeks, Follow-up 24–48h Not 2 Weeks
36 wk infants: 8x ↑ risk TSB >20 mg/dL vs ≥41 wk. Kernicterus Registry (Bhutani + Johnson): nearly ALL late preterm cases were breastfeeding without adequate lactation support + scheduled for 2-week follow-up. นี่คือ preventable catastrophe. Protocol: (1) TcB ก่อน discharge ทุกราย; (2) Bhutani nomogram late preterm thresholds; (3) Follow-up 24–48h after discharge ไม่ใช่ 2 wk; (4) Lactation support mandatory — ไม่ใช่ optional.

PEARL 3 — ENaC + Antenatal Steroids: Mechanism ที่เชื่อม ALPS Trial กับ Bedside
Late preterm มี ↓ ENaC expression (peaks at term) → ↓ lung fluid clearance → TTN/RDS. Glucocorticoids: ↑ ENaC transcription + ↓ channel degradation + ↑ activity → ↑ fluid clearance. ALPS 2016 (Gyamfi-Bannerman NEJM 2016): betamethasone 34–36 6/7 wk → ↓ respiratory complications. ดังนั้น: ถ้า OB ให้ betamethasone ที่ 34–36 wk → expect ↓ TTN/RDS severity แต่ยังคงต้อง monitor — steroids ≠ complete protection.

PEARL 4 — Dextrose Gel: First-Line ก่อน IV Glucose ใน Late Preterm Hypoglycemia
Sugar Babies Study (Harris DL Lancet 2013; RCT, double-blind): 40% dextrose gel 0.5 mL/kg บน buccal mucosa + feed → ↓ NICU admissions + ↓ IV fluids; inexpensive + easy. Gregory 2020: confirmed ↓ IV infusion need. ใช้ก่อน IV ในรายที่ glucose <25 หรือ <35 แต่ยังไม่ symptomatic + ยังดูดกินได้. Symptomatic hypoglycemia หรือ glucose <20 → IV ก่อนเสมอ. Dextrose gel ไม่ใช่ replacement สำหรับ severe/symptomatic cases.

PEARL 5 — Rehospitalization: Late Preterm ที่ไม่เคย NICU มีความเสี่ยงมากที่สุด
Escobar 2005: late preterm ที่ไม่เคย admit NICU → 3x risk rehospitalization vs term. สาเหตุ: clinicians treat them as term → ไม่ทำ proper jaundice risk assessment + discharge too early. นี่คือ systems problem ไม่ใช่แค่ clinical problem. สิ่งที่ต้องทำก่อน discharge: (1) minimum 48h stay; (2) TcB ทุกราย; (3) feeding assessment by lactation-trained staff; (4) 24–48h follow-up scheduled — ไม่ว่า infant จะดูดีแค่ไหน.
13. Evidence Update — Key Studies

Consortium on Safe Labor; Hibbard JU et al. JAMA 2010;304:419: 19 US hospitals; RDS 10.5% at 34 wk → 0.3% at 38 wk; odds of RDS decreased with each advancing week to 38 wk — definitive GA-specific respiratory morbidity data
Tita ATN et al. NEJM 2009;360:111: elective repeat C/S; 37 wk OR 2.1 (1.7–2.5) for adverse outcome vs 39 wk; RDS OR 4.2; NICU admission OR 2.3 — foundational iatrogenic late preterm data

Wang ML et al. Pediatrics 2004;114:372: late preterm vs term: RDS 28.9% vs 4.2% (OR 9.14); temperature instability 10% vs 0%; IV fluids 27% vs 5% (OR 6.48); prolonged stay primary reason feeding failure 76%
Teune MJ et al. Am J Obstet Gynecol 2011;205:374: systematic review 2000–2010; late preterm vs term: RDS RR 17.3; IVH RR 4.9; death <28d RR 5.9; cerebral palsy RR 3.1; 4x mortality first year
Young PC et al. Pediatrics 2007;119:e659: Utah cohort; early neonatal mortality 34 wk = 7.2/1000 (RR 25.5 vs 40 wk); infant mortality 34 wk = 12.5/1000 (RR 10.5) — definitive mortality table
Ely DM, Driscoll AK. Natl Vital Stat Rep 2021;70(14): US 2019 period-linked data; infant mortality 5x higher late preterm vs 39–40 wk (8.2 vs 1.5/1000)

Gyamfi-Bannerman C et al. NEJM 2016;374:1311 (ALPS Trial): RCT betamethasone 34 0/7–36 6/7 wk; ↓ composite respiratory morbidity (primary outcome); changed obstetric practice — ACOG CO 713 (2017): single course betamethasone 34–36 6/7 wk for women at risk of preterm delivery

Harris DL et al. Lancet 2013;382:2077 (Sugar Babies Study): RCT double-blind; dextrose gel 40% buccal; ↓ NICU admissions + ↓ IV fluids; recommended first-line for late preterm + term hypoglycemia <24h
Gregory K et al. Arch Dis Child Fetal Neonatal Ed 2020;105:F45: incorporating dextrose gel + feeding → ↓ IV infusion need — supports widespread adoption
Woythaler MA et al. Pediatrics 2011;127:e622: ECLS-B cohort; late preterm: MDI <70 OR 1.51 (1.26–1.82); PDI <70 OR 1.56 (1.29–1.88) at 24 months
Woythaler M et al. Pediatrics 2015;136:424: same ECLS-B cohort at kindergarten; late preterm still worse Total School Readiness Score — delays persist beyond infancy
Talge NM et al. Pediatrics 2010;126:1124: 336 matched pairs; late preterm at 6 years: ↓ IQ + ↑ teacher-reported behavioral problems (OR significant)
Townley Flores C et al. J Pediatr 2021;232:31: California 73,000 children; moderate + late preterm (32–36 6/7 wk) → ↑ low math/English performance, ↑ chronic absenteeism, ↑ suspension grades K-12
Escobar GJ et al. Arch Dis Child 2005;90:125: 2x ↑ rehospitalization (4.4% vs 2%); never-NICU late preterm: 3x risk readmission vs term; hyperbilirubinemia 63–71% reason
McKinlay CJD et al. JAMA Pediatr 2017;171:972: neonatal hypoglycemia → ↑ risk poor executive function + visual motor function at 4.5 years — supports aggressive glucose management
Bhutani VK, Johnson L. Semin Perinatol 2006;30:89: Kernicterus Registry: late preterm at ↑ risk kernicterus at bilirubin ≤ term thresholds; nearly all breastfeeding without support + 2-wk follow-up
Lapillone A et al. JPGN 2019;69:259 (ESPGHAN): position paper on feeding late + moderately preterm; breastfeeding strongly encouraged; iron 1–2 mg/kg/day <2500g for ≥6mo; vitamin D 400 IU/day
Ninan K et al. JAMA Pediatr 2022:e220483: systematic review + meta-analysis: long-term outcomes of antenatal corticosteroid exposure preterm; no significant long-term harm — supports ALPS findings
14. Self-Check Q&A
Q: นิยาม late preterm คืออะไร และทำไมถึงเปลี่ยนจาก 'near-term' มาเป็น 'late preterm' ในปี 2005?

ตอบ: Late preterm = 34 0/7 – 36 6/7 weeks' gestation (วันที่ 239–259 จาก LMP วันแรก) ตาม NICHD Expert Panel 2005. เปลี่ยนจาก 'near-term' เพราะ: คำว่า near-term ให้ความรู้สึกว่าใกล้เคียง term และ mature — ทำให้ underestimate risk. คำว่า late preterm สื่อว่ายังเป็น preterm และมี risk จาก immaturity ชัดเจนกว่า.
Q: Mechanism ของ respiratory distress ใน late preterm infant ที่เกิดจาก C/S ก่อน onset of labor คืออะไร?

ตอบ: ↓ ENaC expression ใน late preterm → ↓ alveolar Na⁺/water clearance → ↓ fetal lung fluid absorption หลังคลอด. เพิ่มเติม: C/S ก่อน onset of labor → ไม่มี catecholamine surge จาก labor → ↓ ENaC activation; ↓ glucocorticoid surge. ผล: TTN + อาจ RDS แม้ surfactant เพียงพอ. Antenatal betamethasone (ALPS protocol) → ↑ ENaC transcription + ↓ channel degradation → partial correction.
Q: Glucose screening protocol ใน late preterm: target threshold แตกต่างกันอย่างไรตาม age?

ตอบ: AAP (Adamkin 2013): 0–4h: threshold <25 mg/dL; 4–24h: threshold <35 mg/dL; symptomatic any age: <40 mg/dL → IV immediately. เป้าหมาย glucose ≥45 mg/dL ก่อน routine feeds. Pediatric Endocrine Society: goal >50 mg/dL (<48h) และ >60 mg/dL (>48h). Initial feed ภายใน 1h; screen 30 min หลัง feed แรก.
Q: ทำไม late preterm infant ถึงเสี่ยง kernicterus มากกว่า term แม้ที่ bilirubin ระดับเดียวกัน?

ตอบ: Blood-brain barrier ยังไม่สมบูรณ์ ใน late preterm → bilirubin ผ่านเข้า brain ได้ง่ายกว่า. เพิ่มเติม: ↓ hepatic conjugation (↓ UDP-glucuronyl transferase); ↑ enterohepatic circulation; ↓ albumin binding capacity (lower albumin); acidosis + hypoxia (↑ ใน late preterm) → ↑ free bilirubin. Bhutani 2006: kernicterus occurs at lower bilirubin levels in late preterm vs term — ต้องใช้ bilirubin nomogram เฉพาะ high-risk group.
Q: Discharge criteria สำคัญที่สุดสำหรับ late preterm infant มีอะไรบ้าง?

ตอบ: Minimum 48 hours after birth. (1) Vital signs stable 12h; (2) ≥24h successful oral feeding (coordinated suck-swallow-breathe); (3) weight loss ≤7% in 48h; (4) passage ≥1 stool; (5) adequate urine output; (6) pre-discharge bilirubin + nomogram risk assessment; (7) CCHD screen; (8) car seat test; (9) hearing screen; (10) Hep B #1 done; (11) metabolic screen; (12) follow-up appointment 24–48h after discharge (ไม่ใช่ 2 สัปดาห์).
Q: ALPS Trial คืออะไร และเปลี่ยน practice อย่างไร?

ตอบ: ALPS = Antenatal Late Preterm Steroids Trial. Gyamfi-Bannerman C et al. NEJM 2016;374:1311. RCT: betamethasone vs placebo ในมารดาที่ risk of preterm delivery ที่ 34 0/7–36 6/7 wk. Primary outcome: composite respiratory morbidity — significant reduction ใน treated group. เปลี่ยน practice: ACOG CO 713 (2017): แนะนำ single course betamethasone ที่ 34–36 6/7 wk ถ้า risk preterm delivery. Previously: steroids ไม่แนะนำหลัง 34 wk. Mechanism: ↑ ENaC expression → ↑ lung fluid clearance.
Q: Late preterm infant ที่ไม่เคย admit NICU มีความเสี่ยง rehospitalization อย่างไร?

ตอบ: Escobar 2005: late preterm ที่ไม่เคย admit NICU → 3x risk rehospitalization vs term; late preterm ที่ NICU stay <24h → 1.3x risk. สาเหตุ: clinicians treat as term → ไม่ทำ proper jaundice screen, discharge ก่อน feeding established. Escobar: late preterm รวม 4.4% rehospitalized vs 2% term vs 3% <34 wk. Reasons: jaundice 63–71%, feeding 16%, sepsis 6–20%. 'Deceptively normal' appearance = highest risk subgroup.
Q: Dextrose gel มีหลักฐานอะไรรองรับ และใช้อย่างไรในทางปฏิบัติ?

ตอบ: Sugar Babies Study (Harris DL Lancet 2013): RCT double-blind; 40% dextrose gel 0.5 mL/kg massage into buccal mucosa → ↓ NICU admissions + ↓ IV fluids; inexpensive + easy. Gregory 2020: confirmed ↓ IV infusion need. วิธีใช้: 40% dextrose gel 0.5 mL/kg บน buccal mucosa → ตามด้วย breast/formula feed ทันที. ใช้เมื่อ: glucose ต่ำกว่า threshold + infant ยัง feed ได้ + ไม่ symptomatic. ห้ามใช้แทน IV ใน symptomatic hypoglycemia หรือ glucose <20 mg/dL.
Q: Long-term neurodevelopmental outcome ของ late preterm infant มีอะไรบ้าง?

ตอบ: Woythaler 2011: MDI/PDI <70 OR 1.51–1.56 ที่ 24 เดือน; ยังเห็น delay ถึง kindergarten (Woythaler 2015). Talge 2010: ↓ IQ + ↑ behavioral problems ที่ 6 ปี. Townley Flores 2021: ↑ academic failure grades K-12; ↑ suspension (ถึง grade 12). Lindstrom 2007: ↑ disability assistance ในวัยผู้ใหญ่; ↓ มหาวิทยาลัย. Millennium Cohort: ↓ development ทุก domain ที่ 5 ปี. สรุป: effects ไม่ได้จบที่ NICU — ส่งผลตลอดชีวิต.

Day 139 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.42
บทต่อไป: Chapter 42 — Nutritional Support for the Preterm Infant. เตรียมตัว: parenteral nutrition ใน preterm — macronutrient goals (amino acids, glucose, lipid) ตาม GA; early aggressive nutrition vs conservative approach: evidence + metabolic risks; enteral nutrition progression: trophic feeds, full enteral feeds, fortification strategy; human milk vs formula; necrotizing enterocolitis risk reduction: donor HM evidence; metabolic bone disease of prematurity: Ca/P supplementation; growth monitoring goals (g/kg/day, head circumference).
